title: WATCH: Wesley Thomas' Brilliant Testimony at the TX GOP State Convention
author: James Silberman
orientation: A short report on Wesley Thomas' testimony at the 2020 Texas GOP State Convention, where he defended the immediate abolition of abortion against the incrementalist 'Texas Abolition Strategy' and reframed criminalization as the equal protection of the laws applied to preborn human beings.

questions.question: What two competing abortion-related legislative priorities were before the Texas GOP convention committee, and who was behind each?
questions.answer: The committee considered immediate abolition versus the 'Texas Abolition Strategy,' an incremental package put forward by Texas Right to Life that bundled a PRENDA bill, a heartbeat bill, and a partial abolition bill.
questions.quote: The first was abortion's immediate abolition. The other was the "Texas Abolition Strategy," an incremental, Roe-submissive plan put forward by Texas Right to Life centered around a PRENDA bill, a heartbeat bill, and a partial abolition bill.

questions.question: How lopsided was grassroots testimony at the committee hearing between abolitionists and incrementalists?
questions.answer: Testifiers favoring immediate abolition outnumbered the incrementalist witnesses by a five-to-one margin during the committee hearing.
questions.quote: Testifiers in favor of immediate abolition outnumbered incrementalist testimonies five-to-one during the committee hearing.

questions.question: How does Wesley Thomas reframe the charge that abolition bills 'criminalize women'?
questions.answer: Thomas rejects the framing entirely. He argues abolitionists are not pushing to criminalize a class of people but to make specific acts criminal, and that anyone who participates in homicide should be legally culpable.
questions.quote: What I would say is – number one – that we're not pushing for the criminalization of anyone. We're seeking to make criminal certain acts. Anyone involved with homicide should be culpable…

questions.question: When pressed about a 17-year-old girl driven to an abortion facility by her mother, what principle does Thomas appeal to?
questions.answer: He appeals to consistency under existing homicide law: whatever the Texas penal code already does to a 17-year-old who kills her one-year-old, it should do for the same act against a preborn child. The standard is equal treatment, not new or harsher punishment.
questions.quote: If you have a 17-year-old mother who killed her one-year-old child, all we're saying – here's all we're saying – the law as it exists today in the penal code, how the law treats that one-year-old child, it should treat the preborn child exactly the same way.

questions.question: What dual authority does the article cite to ground its demand for equal protection?
questions.answer: The piece grounds equal protection of the preborn in both Scripture and the U.S. Constitution, citing Proverbs 24:10 as a Biblical mandate and the 14th Amendment as a constitutional one.
questions.quote: we are simply demanding the [equal protection of the laws be applied to all human beings](https://freethestates.org/criminalization/index.html). That's a Biblical mandate (Proverbs 24:10) and a Constitutional mandate (14th amendment).

questions.question: Why does the author single out Thomas' testimony for special attention rather than the other abolitionist testimonies?
questions.answer: Because of the live exchange Thomas had with incrementalist committee members—particularly Jonathan Gaspard's pointed questions about criminalizing women—which produced what the author calls a 'fantastic interaction' modeling how to answer the standard objection.
questions.quote: Wesley Thomas' testimony in particular is worth calling attention to here because of the exchanges he had with incrementalist members of the committee. One of the Committee Members, Jonathan Gaspard, asked Thomas about the criminalization of abortion and a fantastic interaction ensued.
